Evaluate shopper journey
Define payment device needs
Decide on device management

Research provider offerings; benchmark competitors; evaluate pros/cons; schedule demo calls
Get internal buy-in; negotiate and agree contract terms

Big Job (JTBD): Validate the planned POS solution to see if it fulfills my business needs
Little Jobs:
Create account structure (test)
Order and set up test devices
Implement integration
Test payment flow
Test payout
Test reconciliation
Micro Jobs:
Create test merchant account and stores; set up payment methods; set up user access
Order test devices; add to cart; add shipping address; track shipping; receive; hardware and security inspection; assign terminals; board terminals; configure settings; configure payment features; upload apps/certificates; schedule deployment
Implement basic integration (PaymentRequest, ReversalRequest, AbortRequest, PaymentResponse, TransactionStatusRequest); implement advanced integration by vertical; implement operational integration; generate API key; create API credential
Test transactions (pay, cancel, refund); test payment methods; test payment features; fix issues
Add payout account; set up payout schedule and model; set payout speed
Check payments list; download report
Key Pain Points:
API credentials management not straightforward
Not all terminals available at all times / terminal shortage
Merchants don't know when terminals will be delivered
Hundreds of possible settings - high learning curve
Boarding not intuitive if terminals not pre-assigned in CA / Mgmt API
Merchants unsure how to configure and afraid of making mistakes
Impact of reboots and maintenance calls is unclear
No overview of all settings or overrides
Technical complexity means merchants need a lot of help from Adyen

Little Jobs:
Set up payment features to allow more transactions (tipping, offline payments)
Ensure payment devices are well distributed across stores

Define which countries and stores have the need; configure settings for selected terminals
Analyze device performance; download terminal list to react; order more devices if needed; return unnecessary devices; redistribute devices to stores with lower transaction volumes

Key Pain Points:

Lack of visibility in available terminal stock (e.g. P400) complicates ordering
No direct Adyen point of contact with IPP expertise slows ordering
Lack of visibility in logistics creates manual overhead
No incentive to recollect inactive terminals from churned sub-merchants
Redistributing terminals not actively supported by Adyen
Resetting terminals to factory settings is not intuitive
Refurbishment not part of Adyen offering

Little Jobs:
Open new stores (existing legal entity)

Big Job (JTBD): Ensure my fleet is always operational
Little Jobs:
Be aware of any issues
Evaluate the severity of the issue
Investigate the issue
Resolve all issues that compromise shoppers' payment experience
Have broken devices replaced
Ensure the same issues won't happen again
Micro Jobs:
Check last activity and assignment status; see notifications
Get immediate support when shoppers are blocked; figure out whether issue is hardware or software; access device details; determine if terminals should be replaced
Troubleshoot functionalities that don't work; troubleshoot misconfiguration; troubleshoot hardware issues (connection, card reader, keypad, NFC)
Contact Adyen Support / AM / Integrators / own company support; reach out to correct people
Document cause and resolution of issues
Key Pain Points:
Merchants not autonomous - rely on support and commercial teams
No proactive way to discover what is going wrong
No immediate support channel for emergencies when shoppers are blocked
Troubleshooting manually done by platforms and time-consuming
Lack of quick support and Adyen expertise when terminal is defective
No access to terminal logs, leading to unnecessary replacements
Insufficient communication from Adyen about recurring problems
Warranty policy insufficient; warranty process cumbersome; Adyen SLA does not match immediate replacement needs
Platforms rely on costly third parties; TRS too expensive; no EDI/API connection; manual handling time-consuming
Accessory replacement not included in TRS - extra costs for platforms
